paragraphs: From July 11 to 15, 2026, I went on a trip to the sea with Bahno and Yongho.

Lately I had been worn down by work and personal feelings. Maybe because of that, I caught a bad cold and my body was slow to recover. As the trip date approached, the three of us set out for the sea, carrying our heavy bodies with us.

After living day after day in the same place, looking at the same scenery and repeating the same routines, just the fact of leaving somewhere made my heart flutter. When I boarded the train and looked out the window, I almost felt as if I could already smell the sea. Imagining the ocean, I listened to music and blankly watched buildings and landscapes rush past.

After we arrived, we walked without a special plan, going out to meet chance. In those chance encounters were people enjoying fireworks, people walking along a festival procession, seagulls flying in flocks across the sky, surfers floating on the waves, and people gazing beyond the sea.

I pressed the shutter without thinking. The moments were so beautiful that they felt less like being recorded on film and more like fireworks—flashing brightly and then vanishing. In those moments, it felt less like reality and more like walking through a dream.
Throughout the trip I smoked, listened to music, and looked up at the sky. Looking at the sky somehow made my worries feel as if they might drift away like clouds, and I felt strangely at ease.
Above all, this trip was better because I was not alone. I had grown used to traveling alone, so sharing the same time with someone else reminded me once again how precious that is.
While traveling, I looked back on my past and tried to embrace even regret and wounds as they were. And I began to look forward a little to a future that has not yet arrived.
In that time, we left photographs of one another. What each of us felt may have been similar or different, but they were moments when something felt connected—as if we had become one.
Please listen to Lee Minhwi’s “A Midsummer Fantasia.”
